game_name: Zork I: The Great Underground Empire

design_philosophy.core_loop: Explore hundreds of interconnected locations, solve environmental puzzles with multiple solutions, collect treasures, interpret natural language commands to interact with world
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Discovery vs. puzzle-solving friction; resource management (inventory weight limits); environmental hazards (darkness/grues); antagonistic NPCs (thief stealing items)
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Explorer-adventurer navigating a mysterious abandoned underground empire, solving riddles through observation and experimentation, accumulating treasure and knowledge
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Radically player-driven progression through complex natural language parsing rather than constrained commands; sarcastic Game Master narrator voice establishes authorial personality; puzzles with multiple solution paths reward creativity over linearity; refuses to hold player hand or explain mechanics

difficulty.checkpointing: No explicit checkpointing mentioned; appears to be persistent world state
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: High agency: player controls exploration order, puzzle approach paths, combat/evasion choices (thief encounters), inventory management. Some puzzles have multiple solutions allowing different difficulty approaches

progression.upgrade_system: Item collection and carrying capacity management (weight-based inventory system). Treasure collection drives score progression and completion metric
progression.unlock_mechanism: Puzzle solutions unlock new areas and items; some puzzles require items obtained from solving different puzzles, creating prerequisite chains. Non-linear approach permitted unless passageways explicitly require problem-solving to traverse
replayability_hooks: Multiple solution paths for individual puzzles
Thief NPC creates unpredictable treasure distribution requiring different strategies
Non-linear exploration order enables different completion sequences
Score system provides completion measurement incentive

world_building_techniques: Environmental description through location names and narrator prose
NPC antagonists (thief, grue) establish world atmosphere and danger
Implicit narrative: exploration of abandoned empire suggests historical catastrophe
Sarcastic Game Master narrator voice creates personality and thematic consistency
Puzzle design embeds story (e.g., dam buttons, maze, loud room with acoustic properties)

notable_bosses: Thief NPC: recurring antagonist that steals items, can be fought or evaded, maintains persistent threat
Grue: environmental hazard in dark areas, killed by player carrying light source

danger_signals: Grue attack in darkness without light source
Timed earthquake mechanic in Zork III alters puzzle outcomes
environmental_storytelling: Abandoned Great Underground Empire suggests collapsed civilization
Treasure distribution indicates previous inhabitants and lost wealth
Architectural features (dam, maze, loud room acoustics) suggest deliberate engineering
Multiple treasures scattered throughout world implies scavenging motivation
